items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.
The answer examiners want is: Insulin. “Glucagon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In type 1 diabetes the pancreas does not produce enough:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Insulin. “Glycogen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mass 80 kg and height 2 m — BMI (kg/m²)?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
